top_papers.title: Cross-Session Threats in AI Agents: Benchmark, Evaluation, and Algorithms
top_papers.summary:
AI-agent guardrails are memoryless: each message is judged in isolation, so an adversary who spreads a single attack across dozens of sessions slips past every session-bound detector because only the aggregate carries the payload. We make three contributions to cross-session threat detection.
  (1) Dataset. CSTM-Bench is 26 executable attack taxonomies classified by kill-chain stage and cross-session operation (accumulate, compose, launder, inject_on_reader), each bound to one of seven identity anchors that ground-truth "violation" as a policy predicate, plus matched Benign-pristine and Benign-hard confounders. Released on Hugging Face as intrinsec-ai/cstm-bench with two 54-scenario splits: dilution (compositional) and cross_session (12 isolation-invisible scenarios produced by a closed-loop rewriter that softens surface phrasing while preserving cross-session artefacts).
  (2) Measurement. Framing cross-session detection as an information bottleneck to a downstream correlator LLM, we find that a session-bound judge and a Full-Log Correlator concatenating every prompt into one long-context call both lose roughly half their attack recall moving from dilution to cross_session, well inside any frontier context window. Scope: 54 scenarios per shard, one correlator family (Anthropic Claude), no prompt optimisation; we release it to motivate larger, multi-provider datasets.
  (3) Algorithm and metric. A bounded-memory Coreset Memory Reader retaining highest-signal fragments at $K=50$ is the only reader whose recall survives both shards. Because ranker reshuffles break KV-cache prefix reuse, we promote $\mathrm{CSR\_prefix}$ (ordered prefix stability, LLM-free) to a first-class metric and fuse it with detection into $\mathrm{CSTM} = 0.7 F_1(\mathrm{CSDA@action}, \mathrm{precision}) + 0.3 \mathrm{CSR\_prefix}$, benchmarking rankers on a single Pareto of recall versus serving stability.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper turns cross-session agent attacks into a benchmarked memory-selection problem and proposes a bounded-memory reader with a serving-aware stability metric.
ai.summary_zh: This paper studies a failure mode in agent safety systems: session-local guardrails can miss attacks whose malicious payload is spread across many sessions and only becomes visible after aggregation. It introduces CSTM-Bench, a benchmark with executable cross-session attack taxonomies, matched benign confounders, and two shards designed to separate dilution from harder isolation-invisible cases. The abstract claims that both per-session judging and naive full-log long-context correlation lose roughly half their recall on the harder split, while a bounded-memory Coreset Memory Reader that keeps K=50 high-signal fragments is the only reader whose recall survives across both shards. The systems-relevant idea is CSR_prefix, which measures ordered prefix stability because fragment reshuffling can break KV-cache reuse, and combines that serving constraint with detection quality in the CSTM score. The main missing detail is external validity: the study is small, uses one correlator family without prompt optimization, and the abstract does not fully clarify baseline fairness or runtime cost tradeoffs.
ai.background_zh: Agent guardrails are typically evaluated per message or per session, which leaves a blind spot when an adversary distributes the payload across interactions and only the aggregate violates policy.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to detect cross-session attacks that are invisible in isolation without assuming that concatenating the entire history into one long-context call is either sufficient or operationally cheap.
ai.approach_zh: It builds a cross-session benchmark, measures session-bound and full-log baselines, and proposes a bounded-memory Coreset Memory Reader plus a combined detection-and-prefix-stability metric that penalizes rankers that disrupt KV-cache-friendly ordering.
ai.evidence_zh: From the abstract alone, the evidence is limited to two 54-scenario shards and one Claude-based correlator family, where baseline recall reportedly drops sharply on the harder split while the K=50 Coreset Memory Reader remains robust; broader generality is not made clear.
ai.value_zh: If the method transfers, it gives agent builders a way to evaluate memory pipelines where security recall and serving stability are optimized together instead of treating long-context aggregation as a free fix.
open_questions: What exact scoring, selection, and ordering procedure does the Coreset Memory Reader use, and how sensitive are results to K and the memory budget?
Were the session-bound, full-log, and coreset baselines matched on prompt quality, token budget, truncation rules, and other factors needed for a fair comparison?
Do the reported gains persist across other correlator models, providers, and more realistic agent logs beyond the small benchmark shards?
core_contributions: CSTM-Bench: a benchmark with 26 executable cross-session attack taxonomies, seven identity anchors, matched benign controls, and two evaluation shards.
An empirical claim that both per-session judging and naive full-log correlation degrade substantially on harder cross-session attacks even within modern context windows.
A bounded-memory Coreset Memory Reader and the CSR_prefix/CSTM metrics that connect detection quality to serving-stable prefix ordering.
why_read: It treats cross-session security as a memory-system design problem rather than only a classifier or prompt-engineering problem.
It makes prefix stability and KV-cache reuse explicit evaluation targets, which is rare in agent security work.
It offers a benchmark structure with hard benign confounders that could be reused to stress-test agent memory and correlation pipelines.
risks: The benchmark is modest in size and partly constructed, so real-world coverage is uncertain.
The evaluation uses one correlator family and no prompt optimization, leaving baseline strength and model dependence unresolved.
recommended_for: Researchers working on agent memory, guardrails, and multi-session threat detection
Serving engineers interested in retrieval order, prefix reuse, and KV-cache stability
Teams evaluating whether bounded-memory readers can outperform naive long-context aggregation
ai.reading_priority_reason: Medium because the overall score is strong and the paper has a real serving-memory angle, but its main contribution is agent security benchmarking rather than core inference acceleration, compiler work, or low-level code generation.
ai.fallback_summary: AI-agent guardrails are memoryless: each message is judged in isolation, so an adversary who spreads a single attack across dozens of sessions slips past every session-bound detector because only the aggregate carries the payload.

top_papers.title: Distributed Generative Inference of LLM at Internet Scales with Multi-Dimensional Communication Optimization
top_papers.summary: Decentralized LLM inference distributes computation among heterogeneous nodes across the internet, offering a performant and cost-efficient solution, alternative to traditional centralized inference. However, the low cross-node network bandwidth makes communication the primary bottleneck. In this paper, we introduce BloomBee, an internet-scale distributed LLM inference framework. BloomBee integrates LLM-layer assignment, micro-batching and tensor offloading to optimize communication from multiple dimensions. Additionally, BloomBee formulates the coordination of these techniques as an optimization problem and solves it using dynamic programming. BloomBee also customizes lossless compression and speculative decoding according to low-bandwidth network settings to reduce communication overhead. We evaluate BloomBee across a spectrum of network environments and show that it improves service throughput by up to 1.76x. It also reduces average latency by up to 43.20% compared to state-of-the-art decentralized LLM inference systems. BloomBee is open-sourced.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper presents BloomBee, a decentralized LLM inference system that attacks the internet-scale bandwidth bottleneck by jointly optimizing partitioning, batching, offloading, compression, and speculative decoding.
ai.summary_zh: BloomBee targets decentralized LLM inference across heterogeneous internet nodes, where cross-node bandwidth rather than raw compute becomes the dominant systems bottleneck. The core claim is that communication must be optimized jointly, so the system combines LLM layer assignment, micro-batching, tensor offloading, lossless compression, and speculative decoding instead of tuning them in isolation. It formulates the coordination of these choices as an optimization problem and solves it with dynamic programming, which is the main mechanism described in the abstract. The reported evidence is an evaluation across multiple network environments with up to 1.76x higher throughput and up to 43.20% lower average latency than prior decentralized inference systems. The main missing details are the hardware and network assumptions, baseline fairness, per-component attribution, and the operational costs of the added coordination logic.
ai.background_zh: Decentralized LLM inference is attractive because heterogeneous internet nodes could provide cheaper and more distributed serving capacity, but wide-area bandwidth is usually the limiting resource.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to make internet-scale decentralized LLM inference practical when low cross-node bandwidth makes communication overhead dominate end-to-end latency and throughput.
ai.approach_zh: BloomBee jointly optimizes layer placement, micro-batching, tensor offloading, lossless compression, and speculative decoding, and coordinates these decisions with a dynamic-programming-based optimizer tailored to low-bandwidth settings.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract claims evaluation across a range of network environments and reports up to 1.76x throughput improvement and up to 43.20% average latency reduction over state-of-the-art decentralized inference systems, but it does not make the setup details clear.
ai.value_zh: If the joint optimization is robust, the system could make bandwidth-limited, heterogeneous, internet-scale LLM serving materially more efficient than current decentralized approaches.
open_questions: What objective, constraints, and system model does the dynamic programming solver use, especially for heterogeneous compute, memory, and link bandwidth?
Which decentralized inference baselines are compared, and are model size, hardware mix, batching policy, and network conditions matched fairly?

How much of the reported gain comes from layer assignment, offloading, compression, and speculative decoding respectively, and what overheads or output-quality constraints does the speculative path introduce?
core_contributions: A decentralized LLM inference framework aimed at internet-scale heterogeneous nodes where communication is the first-order bottleneck.
A joint optimization formulation that coordinates layer assignment, micro-batching, and tensor offloading rather than treating them as separate heuristics.
Network-aware integration of lossless compression and speculative decoding to reduce communication overhead under low-bandwidth conditions.
why_read: It focuses on WAN-bandwidth-limited LLM serving, which is a distinct systems regime from standard datacenter inference work.
The paper claims gains from coordinating several system levers together, making it relevant for readers interested in cross-layer inference optimization.
The abstract reports concrete throughput and latency improvements, so the full paper may expose useful design tradeoffs for decentralized serving.
risks: The abstract does not separate the effect of each optimization, so the true source of the gains is not yet clear.
Internet-scale performance may depend on unstated assumptions about node stability, placement, and network variability that do not generalize cleanly to other deployments.
recommended_for: Researchers working on decentralized or bandwidth-constrained LLM inference systems.
Engineers studying joint scheduling, partitioning, and offloading for heterogeneous inference deployments.
Readers interested in speculative decoding as a systems optimization rather than only a decoding algorithm.
ai.reading_priority_reason: This is a strong fit for LLM inference systems work because it targets communication-limited serving directly, proposes a joint systems optimization rather than a single trick, and reports concrete latency and throughput gains, even though the abstract leaves baseline fairness and deployment assumptions unclear.
ai.fallback_summary: Decentralized LLM inference distributes computation among heterogeneous nodes across the internet, offering a performant and cost-efficient solution, alternative to traditional centralized inference.

top_papers.title: LightSeq: A High Performance Inference Library for Sequence Processing and Generation
top_papers.summary: Describes a CUDA-based high-performance inference library for transformer-family models, emphasizing coarse-grained fusion, dynamic memory reuse, and efficient autoregressive decoding.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper presents a CUDA inference library for transformer-family sequence models that combines coarse-grained fusion, dynamic memory reuse, and optimized autoregressive decoding to reduce serving overhead.
ai.summary_zh: Transformer inference is often limited by kernel launch overhead, memory pressure, and token-by-token decoding costs, especially in sequence generation workloads. This paper presents LightSeq, a CUDA-based inference library for transformer-family models, and centers its design on coarse-grained fusion, dynamic memory reuse, and efficient autoregressive decoding. From the abstract, the contribution is a systems runtime/library effort rather than a new model, aimed at making sequence processing and generation run more efficiently on GPU. The practical value is clear if the implementation is solid: these mechanisms could improve latency and throughput for transformer serving. The biggest missing detail is evaluation evidence, since the abstract does not make the hardware setup, baselines, exact fused operations, or gain breakdown clear.
ai.background_zh: By 2020, transformer inference had become a GPU systems problem, with serving cost increasingly shaped by launch overhead, memory movement, and inefficient incremental decoding rather than model architecture alone.
ai.problem_zh: The paper targets how to build a practical inference runtime for transformer-family sequence models that reduces overhead in both full-sequence processing and autoregressive generation.
ai.approach_zh: Its approach is a CUDA library design that combines coarse-grained operator fusion, dynamic reuse of intermediate memory, and a decoding path specialized for autoregressive generation.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract supports that the paper implements these runtime mechanisms for transformer-family inference, but it does not make the evaluation setup or the strength of the performance evidence clear.
ai.value_zh: If the design works as claimed, it offers a useful systems reference for GPU transformer serving, especially around fusion boundaries, memory planning, and decode-loop efficiency.
open_questions: What hardware, model variants, batch sizes, and sequence lengths are used for evaluation, and are the baselines fair?
Which operators are fused, and how much of the reported speedup comes from fusion versus memory reuse versus decoding-path changes?
How well does the memory reuse and decoding design handle variable sequence lengths, beam search, or broader serving scenarios beyond the core setup?
core_contributions: A CUDA-based inference library for transformer-family sequence processing and generation.
A runtime design centered on coarse-grained fusion and dynamic memory reuse.
An implementation path aimed at more efficient autoregressive decoding.
why_read: It is directly about GPU inference runtime design for transformer generation rather than model changes.
It bundles three recurring serving ideas: kernel fusion, memory planning, and decode optimization.
It may serve as an early systems reference point for later LLM inference stacks.
risks: The abstract does not say which parts of the stack are fused or how portable the fused kernels are.
Performance claims cannot be assessed from the abstract because hardware, baselines, and gain breakdowns are missing or unclear.
recommended_for: Researchers studying transformer inference runtimes and decode-path optimization.
Engineers working on CUDA serving libraries and kernel fusion.
Readers tracing early systems ideas behind later LLM inference stacks.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit with the current focus because it is a CUDA transformer inference library centered on fusion, memory reuse, and autoregressive decoding, though the abstract is too sparse to judge experimental strength without a full read.
ai.fallback_summary: Describes a CUDA-based high-performance inference library for transformer-family models, emphasizing coarse-grained fusion, dynamic memory reuse, and efficient autoregressive decoding.

top_papers.title: Sparse Forcing: Native Trainable Sparse Attention for Real-time Autoregressive Diffusion Video Generation
top_papers.summary: We introduce Sparse Forcing, a training-and-inference paradigm for autoregressive video diffusion models that improves long-horizon generation quality while reducing decoding latency. Sparse Forcing is motivated by an empirical observation in autoregressive diffusion rollouts: attention concentrates on a persistent subset of salient visual blocks, forming an implicit spatiotemporal memory in the KV cache, and exhibits a locally structured block-sparse pattern within sliding windows. Building on this observation, we propose a trainable native sparsity mechanism that learns to compress, preserve, and update these persistent blocks while restricting computation within each local window to a dynamically selected local neighborhood. To make the approach practical at scale for both training and inference, we further propose Persistent Block-Sparse Attention (PBSA), an efficient GPU kernel that accelerates sparse attention and memory updates for low-latency, memory-efficient decoding. Experiments show that Sparse Forcing improves the VBench score by +0.26 over Self-Forcing on 5-second text-to-video generation while delivering a 1.11-1.17x decoding speedup and 42% lower peak KV-cache footprint. The gains are more pronounced on longer-horizon rollouts, delivering improved visual quality with +0.68 and +2.74 VBench improvements, and 1.22x and 1.27x speedups on 20-second and 1-minute generations, respectively.

ai.one_liner_zh: The paper turns an observed persistent-block attention pattern in autoregressive video diffusion into a trainable sparse attention scheme and GPU kernel that reduce KV-cache cost while improving long-horizon generation quality.
ai.summary_zh: Long-horizon autoregressive video diffusion becomes expensive because attention and KV-cache updates grow with the spatiotemporal history. The paper argues that rollouts repeatedly focus on a persistent subset of salient visual blocks, and uses that observation to build Sparse Forcing, which learns how to compress, preserve, and update those blocks while limiting each local window to a dynamic sparse neighborhood. It pairs this with Persistent Block-Sparse Attention (PBSA), a GPU kernel for sparse attention and memory updates during training and inference. The abstract reports VBench gains over Self-Forcing, 1.11-1.27x decoding speedups, and a 42% lower peak KV-cache footprint, with larger gains on longer 20-second and 1-minute rollouts. The main missing detail is whether these gains hold under clearly specified hardware, batch, resolution, and baseline-optimization conditions.
ai.background_zh: Autoregressive video diffusion increasingly runs into decode-time attention cost and KV-cache growth, so reducing memory traffic and sparse compute overhead is central for long-horizon generation.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks whether the attention history in autoregressive diffusion has enough stable structure to support native sparsity without sacrificing, and possibly improving, long-horizon visual quality.
ai.approach_zh: Sparse Forcing learns a persistent memory over salient blocks plus dynamic local neighborhoods, and PBSA implements the resulting block-sparse attention and cache updates as an efficient GPU kernel.
ai.evidence_zh: According to the abstract, experiments against Self-Forcing show higher VBench, 1.11-1.27x faster decoding, and 42% lower peak KV-cache use, but the abstract does not make the hardware setup or baseline fairness clear.
ai.value_zh: If the claimed sparse structure is robust and the kernel is practical, the method offers a path to lower-latency, lower-memory long-horizon generation by reducing both attention compute and cache-update overhead.
open_questions: What hardware, batch size, resolution, and sequence settings were used for the PBSA speedup claims, and were the dense baselines equally optimized?
How is the trainable sparsity learned in practice, and what extra training cost, stability tradeoff, or hyperparameter sensitivity does it introduce relative to Self-Forcing?
How well do the quality and latency gains transfer across different autoregressive video diffusion models, rollout lengths, and prompt distributions?
core_contributions: Identifies a persistent salient-block pattern and local block-sparse structure in autoregressive diffusion attention rollouts.
Introduces a native trainable sparsity mechanism that compresses, preserves, and updates persistent blocks while restricting local attention to dynamic neighborhoods.
Adds Persistent Block-Sparse Attention as a GPU kernel for sparse attention and memory updates aimed at low-latency, memory-efficient decoding.
why_read: It connects a concrete KV-cache structure hypothesis to an explicit sparse kernel implementation rather than stopping at a modeling claim.
It reports both quality gains and systems gains, which is notable for long-horizon autoregressive generation methods.
It may provide transferable ideas for other cache-heavy autoregressive attention workloads, even though the target domain is video diffusion.
risks: The reported speedups are moderate at short rollouts, so the added sparse-kernel complexity may only pay off clearly at longer horizons.

The abstract does not specify kernel portability, hardware assumptions, or whether the comparison used state-of-the-art dense attention implementations as baselines, which limits confidence in the systems claim from the abstract alone.
recommended_for: Researchers studying sparse KV-cache management and attention patterns in autoregressive generation.
Engineers working on long-horizon inference systems where decode latency and memory footprint are coupled.
Compiler and runtime practitioners interested in block-sparse attention kernels and cache-update fusion.
ai.reading_priority_reason: Strong systems overlap through sparse attention, KV-cache compression, and a dedicated GPU kernel, but the target workload is autoregressive video diffusion rather than LLM inference directly.
ai.fallback_summary: We introduce Sparse Forcing, a training-and-inference paradigm for autoregressive video diffusion models that improves long-horizon generation quality while reducing decoding latency.

daily_brief.overview_zh: Today's main threads are training and inference efficiency, factual reliability and verification. The set is anchored by Cross-Session Threats in AI… and Distributed Generative Inference…. LightSeq: A High Performance… adds another data point on whether the same line of work carries stronger evidence or engineering value.
reading_strategy: Start with Cross-Session Threats in AI… and verify whether it defines the factual reliability and verification problem clearly and exposes a real mechanism.
Then read Distributed Generative Inference… to see whether the novelty comes from model design, training procedure, or evaluation reframing.
Use LightSeq: A High Performance… as a final cross-check on experimental boundaries and practical value.
